questions.question: Let Z_n be the group of integers {0, 1, 2, ..., n-1} with addition modulo n as the group operation. The number of elements in the group Z_2 x Z_3 x Z_4 that are their own inverses is ___________.
questions.to_find: Count the self-inverse elements (elements x with x + x = identity) in the direct product group Z_2 x Z_3 x Z_4.

understand.plain: Each group Z_n uses addition modulo n, so the identity is 0 and the inverse of x is the value that adds with x to give 0. In the product group an element is a triple (x, y, z), and it is its own inverse exactly when each coordinate is its own inverse in its own Z_n. For addition mod n, x is self-inverse means 2x is 0 mod n. Counting how many such x exist in each factor and multiplying gives the answer.

keywords.explain: Z_2 x Z_3 x Z_4 combines triples (x, y, z) with coordinate-wise addition modulo 2, 3 and 4
an element a with a + a = e; here it means 2x is 0 in each coordinate's modulus
keywords.example: in Z_4 the value 2 is self-inverse since 2 + 2 = 4 = 0 mod 4

keywords.explain: the number of self-inverse elements of Z_n equals the number of x with 2x divisible by n
keywords.example: Z_2 has two (0,1), Z_3 has one (0), Z_4 has two (0,2)

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Self-inverse elements in each factor of Z2 x Z3 x Z4'><title>Self-inverse counts per factor</title><desc>In Z2 both 0 and 1 are self-inverse, in Z3 only 0 is, in Z4 only 0 and 2 are; multiplying 2 x 1 x 2 gives 4 self-inverse triples.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe class='sub ink'>0</text><rect x='105' y='55' width='55' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='132' y='81' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='100' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>2 self-inverse</text><text x='320' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Z_3</text><rect x='250' y='55' width='55' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='277' y='81' width='40' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='400' y='81' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>2</text><text x='330' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>1 self-inverse</text><text x='540' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Z_4</text><rect x='475' y='55' width='40' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='495' y='81' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>0</text><rect x='517' y='55' width='40' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='537' y='81' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>1</text><rect x='559' y='55' width='40' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='579' y='81' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2</text><text x='540' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>2 self-inverse</text><text x='310' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>2 x 1 x 2 = 4</text><text x='310' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>4 elements equal their own inverse</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three rows of cells for Z2, Z3, Z4 highlight the self-inverse values (0,1 in Z2; 0 in Z3; 0,2 in Z4); multiplying 2 x 1 x 2 yields 4 self-inverse triples.

given.aim: Count the triples (x, y, z) in Z_2 x Z_3 x Z_4 that equal their own additive inverse.

terms.meaning: the integers {0, ..., n-1} under addition modulo n; identity 0, inverse of x is n-x
terms.connects: each coordinate of the product lives in one such factor

terms.meaning: x is its own inverse when 2x is congruent to 0 modulo n
terms.connects: applied independently to each coordinate

terms.meaning: a triple is self-inverse iff every coordinate is, so the total multiplies the per-factor counts
terms.example: (self in Z_2) x (self in Z_3) x (self in Z_4)
terms.connects: gives the final answer 2 x 1 x 2

terms.meaning: the group identity, always one of the self-inverse elements
terms.connects: included in the count alongside the genuine order-2 elements

given.plan: Use the rule that a product element is self-inverse iff each coordinate is; count solutions of 2x = 0 in Z_2, Z_3, Z_4 separately and multiply.

steps.title: Count self-inverse elements in each factor
steps.formula_raw: x is self-inverse in Z_n  <=>  2x = 0 mod n
steps.apply: Z_2: 2x = 0 mod 2 holds for x = 0 and x = 1, so 2 elements. Z_3: 2x = 0 mod 3 holds only for x = 0 (2*1=2, 2*2=4=1), so 1 element. Z_4: 2x = 0 mod 4 holds for x = 0 and x = 2, so 2 elements
steps.note: for odd n only 0 is self-inverse; for even n exactly two values (0 and n/2) are

steps.title: Multiply the per-coordinate counts
steps.apply: a triple (x, y, z) is self-inverse exactly when each of x, y, z is self-inverse in its own factor; multiplying the independent counts gives 2 * 1 * 2 = 4

solution.result: There are 4 self-inverse elements in Z_2 x Z_3 x Z_4.

questions.formula_note: An element of a direct product is self-inverse iff each coordinate satisfies 2x = 0 in its modulus; counting per factor (2, 1, 2) and multiplying gives 4.

questions.question: A = {0, 1, 2, 3, ...} is the set of non-negative integers. Let F be the set of functions from A to itself. For any two functions f_1, f_2 in F, we define (f_1 (-) f_2)(n) = f_1(n) + f_2(n) for every number n in A. Which of the following is/are CORRECT about the mathematical structure (F, (-)) ? A. (F, (-)) is an Abelian group.  B. (F, (-)) is an Abelian monoid.  C. (F, (-)) is a non-Abelian group.  D. (F, (-)) is a non-Abelian monoid.
questions.to_find: Classify the structure (F, (-)) where functions are combined by pointwise addition of their outputs.

understand.plain: The operation (-) adds two functions value-by-value: the new function's output at n is f_1(n) + f_2(n). Because ordinary integer addition is associative and commutative, this pointwise operation inherits both, and the all-zero function z(n) = 0 acts as an identity (adding it changes nothing). So far that is a commutative (Abelian) monoid. To be a group every function would need an inverse, i.e. a function -f with -f(n) = -(f(n)); but those values are negative whenever f(n) > 0, and negatives are not in A = {0,1,2,...}. So inverses are missing and (F, (-)) is an Abelian monoid, not a group.

keywords.explain: (f_1 (-) f_2)(n) = f_1(n) + f_2(n); the structure of (F, (-)) mirrors integer addition at every point n
keywords.example: if f_1(n)=n and f_2(n)=2 then (f_1 (-) f_2)(n) = n + 2

keywords.explain: z with z(n) = 0 for all n satisfies f (-) z = z (-) f = f, so it is the two-sided identity
an inverse of f would need outputs -(f(n)), which leave A whenever f(n) > 0, so most functions have no inverse
keywords.example: the constant function f(n)=1 would need -1, but -1 is not in A

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Pointwise addition of non-negative integer functions forms an Abelian monoid'><title>(F, +pointwise) is an Abelian monoid</title><desc>Pointwise addition is associative, commutative and has the zero function as identity, but inverses leave the non-negative integers, so it is an Abelian monoid not a group.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='40' width='250' height='40' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='155' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>closure + associativity  (ok)</text><rect x='30' y='90' width='250' height='40' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='155' y='116' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>identity z(n) = 0  (ok)</text><rect x='30' y='140' width='250' height='40' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='155' y='166' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>commutative f(-)g = g(-)f  (ok)</text><rect x='30' y='190' width='250' height='40' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='155' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>inverses -f(n) < 0</text><text x='155' y='226' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>leave A  (fails)</text><text x='450' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>Abelian</text><text x='450' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>monoid</text><text x='450' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>not a group</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A checklist shows closure, associativity, the zero-function identity and commutativity all hold, but inverses fail because -f leaves the non-negative integers, so (F, +) is an Abelian monoid, not a group.

given.aim: Decide whether (F, (-)) is an Abelian/non-Abelian group or monoid.

terms.meaning: the non-negative integers; closed under addition but not under negation
terms.example: 3 in A but -3 not in A
terms.connects: the lack of negatives is exactly why inverses fail

terms.meaning: the set of all functions from A to itself; the elements of the structure
terms.example: f(n) = n^2 and g(n) = 5 are members of F
terms.connects: combined pairwise by the pointwise operation (-)

terms.meaning: (f_1 (-) f_2)(n) = f_1(n) + f_2(n); inherits the laws of integer addition
terms.example: associative and commutative at every n
terms.connects: gives closure, associativity, identity and commutativity

terms.meaning: z(n) = 0 for all n; the two-sided identity of (F, (-))
terms.connects: presence of identity makes it at least a monoid

given.plan: Check the four monoid/group properties on the pointwise sum: closure, associativity, identity (all hold), commutativity (holds), inverses (fail because -f leaves A), then classify.

steps.title: Verify monoid and commutativity properties
steps.apply: for f_1, f_2 in F the values f_1(n)+f_2(n) are non-negative integers, so f_1 (-) f_2 is again in F (closure); integer addition is associative, so (-) is too; the zero function z(n)=0 gives f (-) z = z (-) f = f, the identity. Hence (F, (-)) is a monoid
steps.note: every property is inherited point-by-point from (A, +)

steps.title: Confirm commutativity and rule out inverses
steps.formula_raw: f_1 (-) f_2 = f_2 (-) f_1 (commutes), but f's inverse would need -(f(n)) which leaves A
steps.apply: since f_1(n)+f_2(n) = f_2(n)+f_1(n) for all n, the operation is commutative (Abelian). An inverse of f would need g with f(n)+g(n)=0, forcing g(n) = -(f(n)) < 0 whenever f(n) > 0, which is not in A; so most functions lack inverses and it is NOT a group. Therefore (F, (-)) is an Abelian monoid: A, C, D are wrong and B is correct
steps.note: having an identity but missing inverses is exactly the monoid (not group) situation

questions.formula_note: Pointwise addition is associative, commutative and has the zero function as identity (an Abelian monoid), but inverses -f leave the non-negative integers, so it is not a group; hence only B is correct.

questions.question: The complement(s) of the element 'a' in the lattice shown in the figure (with greatest element I at the top, least element O at the bottom, and elements a, b, c, d, e in between, where O < a < I, O < d < I, O < e < I, and O < c < b < I) is (are) ___________.
questions.answer: b, c, d, e  (every element other than a, I and O is a complement of a)
questions.to_find: Find all elements x for which a OR x = I and a AND x = O (the complements of a).

understand.plain: In a bounded lattice the complement of an element a is any element x whose join (least upper bound) with a is the top I and whose meet (greatest lower bound) with a is the bottom O. Element a sits in its own short chain O < a < I and is incomparable to b, c, d, e. For each of those incomparable elements x, the only common upper bound with a is I (so a OR x = I) and the only common lower bound is O (so a AND x = O). Hence b, c, d and e all qualify as complements of a; I and O themselves do not.

keywords.explain: x is a complement of a when a OR x = I (top) and a AND x = O (bottom)
keywords.example: if a and x share only I above and O below, x complements a

keywords.explain: join is the least upper bound, meet is the greatest lower bound of two elements
keywords.example: a OR d = I and a AND d = O when a, d are incomparable atoms-coatoms

keywords.explain: two elements neither of which is below the other; their join climbs to I and meet drops to O here
keywords.example: a and b are incomparable, so a OR b = I and a AND b = O

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 360' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Lattice Hasse diagram with element a and its complements'><title>Complements of a are b, c, d, e</title><desc>Hasse diagram with top I and bottom O; a, d, e lie in length-two chains O-x-I and c-b form a chain O-c-b-I; a is incomparable to b, c, d, e so each is a complement of a.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='500' y1='180' x2='310' y2='300' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='375' y1='150' x2='375' y2='240' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='375' y1='240' x2='310' y2='300' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='310' cy='60' r='8' class='ink'/><text x='310' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>I</text><circle cx='120' cy='180' r='8' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a</text><circle cx='245' cy='180' r='8' class='ink'/><text x='225' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>d</text><circle cx='375' cy='150' r='8' class='ink'/><text x='395' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>b</text><circle cx='375' cy='240' r='8' class='ink'/><text x='397' y='246' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>c</text><circle cx='500' cy='180' r='8' class='ink'/><text x='520' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>e</text><circle cx='310' cy='300' r='8' class='ink'/><text x='310' y='325' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>O</text><text x='310' y='352' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>complements of a: b, c, d, e</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hasse diagram with top I and bottom O; element a (highlighted) is incomparable to b, c, d and e, so each of b, c, d, e is a complement of a while I and O are not.

given.aim: Determine all complements of element a in the bounded lattice.

terms.meaning: the top of the lattice; the join target a OR x must reach
terms.connects: one of the two equalities defining a complement

terms.meaning: the bottom of the lattice; the meet target a AND x must reach
terms.connects: the other equality defining a complement

terms.meaning: lies in the chain O < a < I and is incomparable to b, c, d, e
terms.example: a is neither above nor below d
terms.connects: its incomparability forces join = I and meet = O with each candidate

terms.meaning: the remaining non-extreme elements, each incomparable to a
terms.example: c sits in the chain O < c < b < I
terms.connects: each turns out to satisfy both complement equalities

given.plan: For each non-extreme element x, compute a OR x and a AND x from the Hasse diagram; collect those with a OR x = I and a AND x = O.

steps.title: State the complement condition
steps.formula_raw: x is a complement of a  <=>  (a OR x = I)  and  (a AND x = O)
steps.apply: the top I and bottom O exist (bounded lattice). We test each non-extreme element x against the two equalities; element a lies in O < a < I and is incomparable to each of b, c, d, e
steps.note: I and O can never be complements of a since a AND I = a and a OR O = a, not O / I

steps.title: Test b, c, d, e (and reject extremes)
steps.formula_raw: for x in {b, c, d, e}: a OR x = I and a AND x = O
steps.apply: since a is incomparable to each of b, c, d, e, the only common upper bound of a and x is I, so a OR x = I, and the only common lower bound is O, so a AND x = O. Thus b, c, d, e are all complements; I and O fail the test, so they are excluded
steps.note: a single element here has four complements, showing this lattice is not distributive (where complements are unique)

solution.result: The complements of a are b, c, d and e.

questions.formula_note: A complement of a needs a OR x = I and a AND x = O; since a is incomparable to b, c, d, e in this lattice, all four satisfy both conditions, so a has complements b, c, d, e (multiple complements signal a non-distributive lattice).

questions.question: Show that the elements of the lattice (N, <=), where N is the set of positive integers and a <= b if and only if a divides b, satisfy the distributive property.
questions.answer: N/A - In the divisibility lattice, meet a AND b = gcd(a, b) and join a OR b = lcm(a, b). Using prime factorisations and the identities min(x, max(y, z)) = max(min(x, y), min(x, z)) coordinate-wise on prime exponents, gcd(a, lcm(b, c)) = lcm(gcd(a, b), gcd(a, c)); hence the lattice is distributive.
questions.to_find: Prove that the divisibility lattice (N, |) is distributive: gcd(a, lcm(b,c)) = lcm(gcd(a,b), gcd(a,c)).

understand.plain: In the divisibility order, the meet of two numbers is their gcd and the join is their lcm. The trick is to look at each prime separately. Write the exponent of a prime p in a, b, c as x, y, z. Then gcd takes the smaller exponent (min) and lcm takes the larger (max). Distributivity of the lattice becomes the elementary fact min(x, max(y, z)) = max(min(x, y), min(x, z)), which is true for all real numbers. Since it holds prime by prime, it holds for the whole numbers, so the lattice is distributive.

keywords.explain: a <= b means a divides b; meet is gcd and join is lcm
represent each number by its vector of prime exponents; gcd uses min and lcm uses max coordinate-wise
a lattice where meet distributes over join (and vice versa): a AND (b OR c) = (a AND b) OR (a AND c)

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 620 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Distributivity of the divisibility lattice via min and max of prime exponents'><title>min over max distributivity</title><desc>For each prime, gcd is the min exponent and lcm is the max exponent; the identity min(x, max(y,z)) = max(min(x,y), min(x,z)) holds coordinate-wise, so the divisibility lattice is distributive.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='40' width='270' height='44' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='165' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>meet = gcd  ->  min of exponents</text><text x='165' y='78' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>join = lcm  ->  max of exponents</text><rect x='30' y='110' width='560' height='44' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='310' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>gcd(a, lcm(b,c)) = lcm(gcd(a,b), gcd(a,c))</text><rect x='30' y='180' width='560' height='44' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='208' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>per prime: min(x, max(y,z)) = max(min(x,y), min(x,z))</text><text x='310' y='258' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>holds for every prime  =>  lattice is distributive</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two panels show gcd as min and lcm as max of prime exponents, then the per-prime identity min(x, max(y,z)) = max(min(x,y), min(x,z)) that proves the divisibility lattice is distributive.

given.aim: Prove a AND (b OR c) = (a AND b) OR (a AND c) in the divisibility lattice (N, |).

terms.meaning: positive integers ordered by divisibility; a <= b iff a divides b
terms.connects: the partial order whose meet/join we must identify

terms.meaning: the greatest common divisor is the greatest lower bound under divisibility
terms.connects: the AND operation in the distributive law

terms.meaning: the least common multiple is the least upper bound under divisibility
terms.connects: the OR operation in the distributive law

terms.meaning: exponent of a fixed prime p in a, b, c; gcd uses min, lcm uses max
terms.connects: reduces lattice distributivity to min/max distributivity

given.plan: Identify meet with gcd and join with lcm, pass to prime exponents so gcd/lcm become min/max, then prove min(x, max(y,z)) = max(min(x,y), min(x,z)) holds for each prime.

steps.title: Reduce gcd/lcm to min/max of prime exponents
steps.formula_raw: a AND b = gcd(a,b), a OR b = lcm(a,b); exponent of p in gcd = min, in lcm = max
steps.apply: for each prime p let x, y, z be its exponents in a, b, c. Then the exponent of p in gcd(a, lcm(b,c)) is min(x, max(y, z)), and in lcm(gcd(a,b), gcd(a,c)) is max(min(x, y), min(x, z))
steps.note: two numbers are equal iff their prime exponents agree, so it suffices to match exponents prime by prime

steps.title: Prove the min-over-max identity
steps.formula_raw: min(x, max(y, z)) = max(min(x, y), min(x, z)) for all real x, y, z
steps.apply: min distributes over max (a standard lattice identity for totally ordered numbers): checking the orderings of x, y, z confirms both sides equal the median-type value. Since this holds for every prime exponent, gcd(a, lcm(b,c)) = lcm(gcd(a,b), gcd(a,c)) for all a, b, c, so meet distributes over join
steps.note: the dual law lcm(a, gcd(b,c)) = gcd(lcm(a,b), lcm(a,c)) follows the same way using max(x, min(y,z)) = min(max(x,y), max(x,z))

solution.result: gcd(a, lcm(b,c)) = lcm(gcd(a,b), gcd(a,c)) for all a, b, c, so (N, |) is a distributive lattice.

questions.formula_note: Meet is gcd and join is lcm; passing to prime exponents turns distributivity into min(x, max(y,z)) = max(min(x,y), min(x,z)), which holds for every prime, proving (N, |) distributive. The official key marks this proof item as N/A (descriptive).

questions.question: Consider the set of integers {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24} together with the two binary operations LCM (lowest common multiple) and GCD (greatest common divisor). Which of the following algebraic structures does this represent? A. group  B. ring  C. field  D. lattice
questions.to_find: Classify the structure (set of divisors of 24, LCM, GCD).

understand.plain: The set {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24} is exactly the set of all positive divisors of 24. Ordered by divisibility, any two of these numbers have a greatest common divisor (GCD) inside the set and a lowest common multiple (LCM) inside the set, and both stay within the divisors of 24. Two operations giving a meet (GCD) and a join (LCM) that satisfy the lattice laws make this a lattice. It is not a group, ring or field: GCD and LCM have no inverses (you cannot 'undo' a GCD), so the group-style structures do not apply.

keywords.explain: the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24} is all positive numbers dividing 24, written D_24

keywords.term: GCD as meet, LCM as join
keywords.explain: under divisibility, GCD is the greatest lower bound (meet) and LCM is the least upper bound (join)
keywords.example: GCD(8, 12) = 4 and LCM(8, 12) = 24, both in the set

keywords.explain: a partially ordered set in which every pair has a meet and a join; closure of GCD/LCM here makes D_24 a lattice
keywords.example: 1 is the bottom, 24 is the top of this lattice

understand.visual_alt: Hasse diagram of the divisors of 24 (1 at the bottom up to 24 at the top) showing that GCD acts as meet and LCM as join, so the structure is a lattice.

given.aim: Identify the algebraic structure formed by the divisors of 24 with LCM and GCD.

terms.meaning: all positive divisors of 24 (the set D_24)
terms.connects: the carrier set ordered by divisibility

terms.meaning: greatest common divisor; the meet (greatest lower bound) under divisibility
terms.connects: the meet of the lattice; closed in the set

terms.meaning: least common multiple; the join (least upper bound) under divisibility
terms.connects: the join of the lattice; closed in the set

terms.meaning: 1 divides everything (bottom) and 24 is divisible by everything in the set (top)
terms.connects: the universal lower and upper bounds of the lattice

given.plan: Recognise the set as the divisors of 24, verify GCD/LCM act as meet/join with closure, and check why it is a lattice rather than a group, ring or field.

steps.title: Recognise GCD/LCM as meet and join
steps.formula_raw: a AND b = GCD(a,b), a OR b = LCM(a,b) on the divisors of 24
steps.apply: ordering the set {1,2,3,4,6,8,12,24} by divisibility makes GCD the greatest lower bound and LCM the least upper bound; both results are again divisors of 24, so the set is closed under meet and join with bottom 1 and top 24
steps.note: every pair of divisors of n always has its GCD and LCM among the divisors of n

steps.title: Eliminate group, ring, field
steps.formula_raw: GCD and LCM have no inverses, so no group/ring/field structure
steps.apply: a group/ring/field needs inverse elements, but you cannot invert a GCD or LCM (e.g. nothing 'un-GCDs' back to a chosen value); the two operations only provide a bounded meet-join structure. Hence the structure is a lattice, not a group (A), ring (B) or field (C)
steps.note: GCD and LCM are idempotent (GCD(a,a)=a), unlike addition or multiplication in a ring

solution.result: The structure is a lattice (Option D).

questions.formula_note: The divisors of 24 are closed under GCD (meet) and LCM (join) with bottom 1 and top 24, giving a lattice; the absence of inverses rules out group, ring and field, so the answer is D.

formulas.name: Complement of an Element in a Bounded Lattice
formulas.latex: x \text{ is a complement of } a \iff a \vee x = I \text{ and } a \wedge x = O
formulas.plain: in a bounded lattice with top I and bottom O, an element x is a complement of a when their join is the top (a OR x = I) and their meet is the bottom (a AND x = O); an element can have zero, one, or several complements
formulas.whenToUse: to find or verify complements of an element in a bounded lattice, e.g. reading a Hasse diagram

terms.means: the greatest (top) element of the lattice
the least (bottom) element of the lattice
the join (least upper bound) and meet (greatest lower bound) of a and x

formulas.trap: complements need not be unique; a single element can have several complements, and that is exactly what happens in a non-distributive lattice (in a distributive lattice each element has at most one complement)
formulas.reference: Bounded and complemented lattices; order theory

formulas.name: Divisibility Lattice (GCD as Meet, LCM as Join)
formulas.plain: under the divisibility order (a <= b iff a divides b) the meet of two numbers is their gcd and the join is their lcm; on the set of divisors of a fixed n this is a closed, distributive lattice with bottom 1 and top n
formulas.whenToUse: to identify or analyse a lattice built from integers ordered by divisibility, or the divisors of a number under gcd/lcm

terms.means: greatest common divisor = meet = min of prime exponents
least common multiple = join = max of prime exponents
the bottom (divides all) and top (divisible by all divisors) of the divisor lattice of n

formulas.trap: gcd and lcm have no inverses, so the structure is a lattice, not a group/ring/field; the divisibility lattice is always distributive (min/max distribute coordinate-wise on prime exponents)
formulas.reference: Divisor lattices; meet and join under divisibility, order theory
